access.format: HTML full text generated from structured XML
notes: The current item is indexed publicly even though unattended direct requests from this research environment received HTTP 403 responses.

rights.basis: The current U-M item and collection pages state that the materials are in the public domain in the United States and apply the No Copyright-United States statement.
rights.component: University of Michigan CME digital text and markup
notes: The XML preserves a legacy availability statement permitting individual personal, research, teaching, and linking uses while forbidding redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers. The current public-domain statement appears broader, but the inspected pages do not explicitly explain whether the legacy clause was superseded; downstream users should preserve the conflict rather than silently choose one.

entities.description: A 1999 manually keyed and reviewed encoding of the 1969 Greenwood reprint. It preserves Furnivall's front matter, two complete witness texts, notes, printed-page breaks, and manuscript-leaf milestones, while linearizing parallel print and excluding the glossary and indexes.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item EngConIre
notes: The header says the text was keyboarded to an error-rate target of no more than one error per 20,000 characters, then 5 percent was reviewed before Kenneth Church marked it up and Catherine E. Paul reviewed the markup.

The current U-M collection page states No Copyright-United States, while the legacy XML header preserves an older reuse clause that permits individual personal, research, and teaching use but forbids redistribution by content providers.

rights.basis: The current U-M item and collection pages state that the materials are in the public domain in the United States and apply the No Copyright-United States statement.
rights.component: University of Michigan CME digital text and markup
notes: The XML preserves a legacy availability statement permitting individual personal, research, teaching, and linking uses while forbidding redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers. The current public-domain statement appears broader, but the inspected pages do not explicitly explain whether the legacy clause was superseded; downstream users should preserve the conflict rather than silently choose one.

dateStatements.label: Secure first attestation from a currently dated source witness
dateStatements.notes: This is a terminus from a witness date, not an exact translation date.
dateStatements.value: By 1400-1410, the present catalog date of MS. Rawl. B. 490

entities.description: The abstract Hiberno-Middle English prose translation, abridgment, and adaptation represented by multiple manuscript copies. It is one work across divergent witnesses, not Gerald of Wales's Latin work, a manuscript object, or Furnivall's two-column edition.
entities.label: Anonymous Middle English English Conquest of Ireland
notes: Modern scholarship treats the lost original translation as a Hiberno-English version made in Ireland for a colonial English audience and surviving in several fifteenth- and sixteenth-century copies.

The translation is shorter than the Latin work, reorders and omits material, enlarges selected passages, and appears to depend on an intermediate recension; no physical Latin exemplar has been identified.

rights.basis: The source work and anonymous translation are medieval. This conclusion does not extend to modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, catalog metadata, or manuscript photographs.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English wording and Gerald's twelfth-century source work

entities.description: Gerald of Wales's Latin history of the Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland. This abstract source-work entity does not identify the particular Latin manuscript or recension used by the anonymous Middle English translator.
entities.label: Gerald of Wales, Expugnatio Hibernica

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185, EETS Original Series 107 (New York: Greenwood Press, 1969), reprint of the 1896 edition.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 107
bibliographic.title: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185

entities.description: The immediate print source named in the U-M XML header: a 1969 EETS Old Series 107 reprint of Furnivall's 1896 edition.
entities.label: Greenwood Press reprint of The English Conquest of Ireland (1969)

rights.basis: The underlying 1896 edition is public domain, but no inspected 1969 copy or item-specific notice establishes whether Greenwood added protectable matter or asserted separate rights in the reprint layer.
rights.component: 1969 reprint production and any reprint-specific matter

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185: Mainly from the Expugnatio Hibernica of Giraldus Cambrensis, Part I, The Text, Early English Text Society, Original Series 107 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1896).
bibliographic.pages: xvi + 172 printed pages, with an unnumbered correction leaf
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 107
bibliographic.title: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185

entities.description: Furnivall's parallel-text edition of the complete Middle English work in TCD MS 592 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. B. 490, with forewords, extensive Latin comparison notes, a specimen from TCD MS 593, a note on untranslated final Latin chapters, a glossary, and indexes. The U-M encoding omits the glossary and indexes and does not reproduce the Henry Bradley correction leaf visible in the cached 1896 copy.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, The English Conquest of Ireland (1896)
notes: Mrs Parker copied MS. Rawl. B. 490; George Parker collated its proofs against the manuscript; Thomas Austin prepared the glossary and indexes; John Kells Ingram supplied English side-notes to even pp. 10-32, with two or three altered by Furnivall.
Furnivall had TCD MS 592 in hand and read proofs against it. The accessible forewords do not name a separate TCD working-copy maker.

Furnivall says Dimock's notes, dates, and preface were of the greatest use; the many Latin parallels and source discussions therefore belong to the editorial apparatus, not to the Middle English work's language facet.

physicalDescription.extent: Preliminaries through p. xvi; main parallel text and back note pp. 1-152; glossary and indexes pp. 153-172; unnumbered Henry Bradley correction leaf

rights.basis: The book was published in 1896, well before the U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works, and U-M currently identifies the item and collection as public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1896 edition, transcript, introduction, notes, glossary, indexes, and correction leaf

entities.description: The material transcript commissioned by Furnivall from Mrs Parker and used to set the Rawlinson column. It is provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no dedicated working-transcript entity type; it is not a medieval witness.
entities.label: Mrs Parker's working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490

bibliographic.citation: James F. Dimock, ed., Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, vol. V, Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 21 (London: Longmans, Green, Reader, and Dyer, 1867).
bibliographic.title: Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, volume V

entities.description: The Rolls Series Latin edition of the Expugnatio Hibernica consulted by Furnivall for notes, dates, prefatory source analysis, and Latin comparison passages. It is a scholarly intermediary for the apparatus, not the anonymous translator's proven physical exemplar.
entities.label: James F. Dimock, Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, volume V (1867)

rights.basis: The accessible DataCite record has an empty rights list, and CAPTCHA-blocked image delivery prevented inspection of any manifest-level rights statement or image license.
rights.component: TCD digital manuscript images and delivery layer

dateStatements.label: Furnivall's witness date
dateStatements.notes: The accessible current DOI metadata does not supply a normalized manuscript date, so Furnivall's historical dating remains explicitly qualified.

entities.description: The principal Dublin witness printed in Furnivall's even-page column. The English Conquest occupies fols. 1r-27v; fol. 28r contains an unrelated fragment of the dedication to James Yonge's Gouvernaunce of Princes and an incomplete English prayer. Those adjacent items are outside the EngConIre work boundary.

holding.notes: The institutional DOI record links the present catalog entry and documents the old edition context.

entities.label: Dublin, Trinity College Library, IE TCD MS 592
notes: The current TCD prose description calls the work 'by Thomas Bray' and includes James Yonge in creator metadata, but the same record locates Yonge's distinct text only on fol. 28r. Neither name is treated here as confirmed author of the Conquest.
The XML's final Dublin milestone values '42b.' and '7b.' conflict with the otherwise sequential foliation and the current official locus ending at 27v.

physicalDescription.extent: ii + fols. 1-28 + ii; English Conquest on fols. 1r-27v
notes: The DOI description records red and green decoration, marginal sketches, later material on fol. 28r, and a tooled leather binding.
No support material is asserted here because the accessible DOI metadata did not state it.

rights.basis: The accessible DataCite record has an empty rights list, and CAPTCHA-blocked image delivery prevented inspection of any manifest-level rights statement or image license.
rights.component: TCD digital manuscript images and delivery layer

access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490
notes: The current catalog record has no digital-facsimile link. Digital Bodleian API searches for the exact shelfmark, expanded shelfmark, Summary Catalogue number, and work title returned no MS. Rawl. B. 490 object.
This dated negative finding does not prove that the manuscript has never been photographed or that no internal surrogate exists.

access.accessMethod: Register for Bodleian Archives & Manuscripts Requests and order the manuscript to the Weston Library Level 1 reading room, subject to item-specific review.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490

access.accessMethod: Request reference-quality PDF scans through Bodleian special-collections mediated copying; contact mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk.
access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490
notes: For a preservation-complete request, ask first for the repository's verified total foliation and then request the whole physical object: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all front and rear endleaves including blanks, every recto and verso, and any inserted or folded surfaces. Map the corpus work separately to fols. 1r-28r after delivery.

The public mediated-copying page charges non-Oxford users £1 per scan and says legal or conservation restrictions can prevent scanning. Complete-object feasibility and price require an item-specific response.
Delivery does not itself establish permission for repository redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.basis: The current service page says delivered images are subject to Imaging Services terms of use. No item-specific delivery, license, or reuse permission has been received for MS. Rawl. B. 490.
rights.component: Future reference scans supplied through Bodleian mediated copying

dateStatements.label: Furnivall's witness date
dateStatements.notes: Retained as historical edition metadata; it conflicts with the present catalog's 1400-1410 range.

entities.description: A parchment codex containing the English Conquest of Ireland followed by the Middle English Secreta secretorum translated by James Yonge. The Conquest is represented by Furnivall and the XML from fol. 1r through fol. 28r; the current abbreviated catalog does not itself state that locus.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490

notes: The current summary record does not provide a complete foliation, dimensions, collation, binding description, or item-level loci.
Richard Rawlinson owned the codex and bequeathed it to the Bodleian in 1755.

rights.basis: The current service page says delivered images are subject to Imaging Services terms of use. No item-specific delivery, license, or reuse permission has been received for MS. Rawl. B. 490.
rights.component: Future reference scans supplied through Bodleian mediated copying

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: The Conquest section is in a Tudor secretary hand; later sections contain several additional hands.

entities.description: A paper manuscript whose pp. 1-55 contain a later English copy of the Conquest and whose pp. 58-182 contain extracts and notes from other chronicles in multiple sixteenth- and seventeenth-century hands. EngConIre includes only Furnivall's specimen from the middle of MS 593 p. 7, printed on edition pp. xiv-xvi for comparison with chapter XIII.
entities.label: Dublin, Trinity College Library, IE TCD MS 593
notes: Furnivall says this was reported to be a sixteenth-century transcript of E.2.31/MS 592. The currently accessible institutional metadata calls MS 592 an earlier version but does not prove direct copying, so no direct witness-to-witness relation is asserted.

physicalDescription.extent: pp. 1-182; Conquest on pp. 1-55; Furnivall specimen begins at p. 7, middle
notes: The DOI description says the tightly bound manuscript cannot be collated without distress and records a single vase-and-flower watermark.

next_steps: Compare the complete English witness corpus against current Latin-recension scholarship rather than equating the source with Dimock's 1867 edition or Furnivall's Harley 177 comparison.
Consult Caoimhe Whelan's 2015 doctoral thesis and any later stemmatic work if an authorized copy becomes available.
notes: The relation to Expugnatio Hibernica is secure at work level; the physical source chain remains unknown.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin recension and physical exemplar underlie the lost original Middle English translation?

next_steps: Ask TCD Manuscripts & Archives for the source and status of the Thomas Bray attribution in the MS 592 record.
Separate James Yonge's fol. 28r Gouvernaunce of Princes fragment and the Rawlinson Secreta from the anonymous Conquest in all future cataloging.
Review Whelan's thesis and current IPMEP authority data for explicit authorship discussion.
openQuestions.question: What evidence, if any, supports TCD's current 'by Thomas Bray' wording or any attribution of the Conquest to James Yonge?

next_steps: Use all independent witnesses and current dialect/localization scholarship; do not transfer one copyist's dialect or Furnivall's conjectured fourteenth-century date to the archetype.
Review later work on the translation's audience, patronage, and manuscript stemma.
openQuestions.question: Can the date and place of the lost archetypal Hiberno-English translation be narrowed beyond production in Ireland by the early fifteenth-century Rawlinson witness?

next_steps: Open each DOI in a normal interactive browser and inspect the network or download controls after human verification, without bypassing access controls.
Ask TCD Digital Collections for canonical manifests, canvas counts, original-file downloads, and item-level image rights statements.
If public payloads are supplied, cache and audit the complete physical object before extracting the EngConIre work portion.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, licensed image inventories or IIIF manifests for TCD MSS 592 and 593 be obtained without relying on an interactive CAPTCHA?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether prior whole-object photography or microfilm exists and request its complete inventory.
If none exists, request a conservation and price assessment for full-object mediated copying or Imaging Services photography.
Confirm total leaves, endleaves, binding surfaces, and work locus before specifying a complete order.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete internal or requestable digital surrogate of MS. Rawl. B. 490 already exist, and what is the codex's verified total foliation?

next_steps: Compare complete images or diplomatic transcriptions of MS 593 pp. 1-55 with MS 592 fols. 1r-27v, including shared errors and omissions.
Consult the full TCD catalog description and modern stemmatic scholarship before asserting a direct witness-to-witness relation.
openQuestions.question: Is the Conquest in TCD MS 593 demonstrably a direct transcript of TCD MS 592, as reported by Furnivall, or only a later related version?

next_steps: Compare the exact 1969 reprint pages with the 1896 scan and, if possible, TCD MS 592 images before changing source XML.
Treat current XML values as representation data and the official 1r-27v locus as catalog evidence until a separate correction task is authorized.
openQuestions.question: Should the Dublin milestone values 42b and final 7b be corrected to 24v and 27v, and did the 1969 source copy include Henry Bradley's correction leaf?

next_steps: Search Furnivall and EETS archival finding aids for Rawlinson B. 490, Mrs Parker, and George Parker correspondence or proofs.
Do not infer the transcript's loss merely from its absence in the inspected edition and online catalogs.
openQuestions.question: Does Mrs Parker's working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490 survive in Furnivall, EETS, Clarendon Press, or Parker papers?

relations.subjectLabel: Internet Archive complete scan of Furnivall's 1896 edition

reviewNotes: The current TCD crosswalk is E.2.31 = IE TCD MS 592 and F.4.4 = IE TCD MS 593. MS 593 contributes only Furnivall's pp. xiv-xvi specimen and is not a source for either complete body stream.
The XML HEADER's AUTHOR value for Giraldus Cambrensis is inherited edition-level cataloging. It does not make Gerald the author of the anonymous Middle English wording.
The CME submodule revision is e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c. The exact EngConIre.xml git blob is 31c3b76dd2c44ae2a6f881446c8da8bf7d8966b4; these two identifiers are not interchangeable.
The present Bodleian catalog dates MS. Rawl. B. 490 to 1400-1410, whereas Furnivall dated it about 1440. Both statements are retained at their own evidence layers.
No complete manuscript-image bundle is present in source-cache/EngConIre. The TCD JSON files and Bodleian TEI file are metadata-only research copies.

lineage.summary: The checked-in EngConIre XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan's 1999 encoding of the 1969 Greenwood reprint of Frederick J. Furnivall's 1896 EETS parallel-text edition. Furnivall printed the complete Middle English work in two witness versions: Trinity College Dublin MS 592, formerly E.2.31, and Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490. The Rawlinson path runs through a working transcript made by Mrs Parker and proofs collated against the manuscript by George Parker; the edition also prints a narrowly scoped specimen from TCD MS 593, formerly F.4.4, and consults James F. Dimock's 1867 Latin edition for source comparison and notes. The abstract Middle English work is an anonymous, freely abridged and rearranged Hiberno-English version of Gerald of Wales's Expugnatio Hibernica; its exact Latin exemplar is unidentified. A complete scan of the 1896 edition is cached with OCR and provider metadata. TCD's records describe digitized versions of MSS 592 and 593, but automated image or IIIF retrieval was blocked by an interactive CAPTCHA, so the cached DOI records are metadata only and no TCD facsimile is asserted. No public Digital Bodleian object for Rawl. B. 490 was found in a dated API search; reading-room and paid complete-object copying remain request routes.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter
catalogSummary.date: Translation date unknown; currently attested by MS. Rawl. B. 490, dated 1400–1410, and therefore composed no later than 1410

catalogSummary.title: English Conquest of Ireland

catalogingSubject.label: English Conquest of Ireland
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is one anonymous late-medieval Hiberno-Middle English prose translation and free adaptation of Gerald of Wales's Latin Expugnatio Hibernica. EngConIre represents that work in two parallel manuscript versions printed on facing pages by Frederick J. Furnivall and serialized sequentially in the University of Michigan XML. The record does not identify the anonymous English translator with Gerald of Wales, Thomas Bray, James Yonge, any manuscript scribe, Furnivall, either physical codex, the 1896 or 1969 edition, the Michigan encoding, or the General Corpus XML artifact. Furnivall's preliminaries, his specimen from a third manuscript, and his terminal note are retained as separately labeled editorial paratext, not counted as additional medieval works.

contentParts.label: English Conquest of Ireland
notes: The two child parts are versions of this one work and therefore have zero item counts.
contentParts.summary: The single anonymous Hiberno-Middle English prose work, counted once although represented in two complete witness versions.

contentParts.label: TCD MS 592 version
notes: MS 592's adjacent f. 28r fragment of James Yonge's Secreta Secretorum dedication and a prayer are outside this work boundary.
The XML contains malformed late folio milestone values '42b' and '7b'; these are preserved as encoding defects, not accepted as manuscript foliation.
contentParts.summary: The complete version copied in the manuscript formerly cited as TCD E.2.31 and now cataloged as IE TCD MS 592, printed on the even pages of Furnivall's parallel-text edition.

contentParts.label: MS. Rawl. B. 490 version

locators.value: ff. 1r–28r, inferred from Furnivall's edition and XML folio milestones

notes: The current Bodleian catalog identifies the English Conquest as the manuscript's first work but does not state its precise locus; the folio range here is an edition-to-encoding inference and remains qualified.
The manuscript also contains James Yonge's Secreta Secretorum translation, which is a separate adjacent work.
contentParts.summary: The complete version in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490, copied for Furnivall by Mrs Parker and printed on the odd pages of the parallel-text edition.

contentParts.label: Furnivall's editorial paratext

locators.value: preliminary matter through p. xvi and terminal editorial note

notes: These modern editorial materials are represented because the XML retains them, but their language and prose are not facets or counted items of the medieval work.
contentParts.summary: The retained title matter, dedication, forewords, third-manuscript specimen, and terminal note surrounding the two medieval witness streams.

contentParts.label: Title matter, dedication, and forewords

locators.value: title and dedication; foreword pp. vii–xiii

contentParts.summary: Furnivall's title matter, dedication, account of the manuscripts and collaborators, source discussion, historical framing, and editorial acknowledgments.

contentParts.label: TCD MS 593 comparison specimen

locators.value: pp. xiv–xvi; compared with chapter XIII on pp. 30–35

notes: Furnivall described MS 593 (then F.4.4) as a later transcript of MS 592; the modern TCD record describes the textual relation more cautiously.
contentParts.summary: A short comparison specimen from the middle of TCD MS 593 p. 7, corresponding to chapter XIII in the two main edition witnesses; it is not a third complete encoded version.

contentParts.label: The unenglisht last Chapters
contentParts.summary: Furnivall's terminal editorial note discussing material from the Latin source not rendered in the English versions.

assertion.notes: The upper bound is inferred from the current 1400–1410 date of MS. Rawl. B. 490, not from a dated authorial exemplar. Furnivall's older estimate of about 1440 for that manuscript is retained at witness level in the lineage record.

dateStatements.display: Translation date unknown; composed no later than 1410 on the currently verified evidence

assertion.notes: This is a witness date and terminus ante quem for the work, not a composition date. The full census of eight surviving English texts was not independently redated here.

dateStatements.display: Earliest witness verified in this research: MS. Rawl. B. 490, currently dated 1400–1410
Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland, 1166–1185

extent.description: One medieval prose work, represented in two complete encoded witness versions. Each version has 62 XML DIV2 chapter units, for 124 version-level divisions in the representation; the complete XML has 5 DIV1, 127 DIV2, 130 HEAD, 112 MILESTONE, 171 PB, 185 P, 196 NOTE1, 43 CORR, and 146 SUPPLIED elements. These are encoding and edition metrics, not a claim that the abstract work intrinsically has 124 chapters. The front and back editorial parts are retained with zero item counts, and the glossary and indexes are absent from the XML.

formStatements.description: Continuous vernacular historical prose divided into editorially regularized chapter units in the encoded edition.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The relation is both translational and adaptive: omission, reordering, condensation, and expansion are integral to the vernacular work.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The work's Middle English is more specifically described in scholarship as Hiberno-Middle English. Latin and Greek in Furnivall's notes, quotations, and comparison material belong to editorial or source layers and are deliberately excluded from this work-language facet.
The abstract work is counted once. TCD MS 592 and MS. Rawl. B. 490 are parallel witness-version parts with zero item counts, not two independent works.

Furnivall's title, chapter heads, supplied passages, punctuation, notes, and source comparisons belong to the edition layer. The XML further serializes a facing-page edition and includes explicit normalization and milestone-placement judgments.
The TCD MS 593 passage is only a foreword specimen. It is not modeled as a third complete encoded version and TCD MS 593 is not placed in the main source path.

Gerald of Wales is the author of Expugnatio Hibernica, not the English Conquest. James Yonge is associated with separate adjacent manuscript content, and the current Thomas Bray wording is retained only as disputed attribution history.
Latin and Greek editorial quotations and apparatus are excluded from the work language facets. Encoding element counts are representation metrics, not intrinsic intellectual structure.

The immediate 1969 Greenwood source was not physically inspected. The locally cached complete 1896 scan contains an unnumbered Henry Bradley correction leaf absent from the XML, leaving its presence in the 1969 reprint unresolved at the edition layer.

next_steps: Review the complete witness census and current dialectological and textual scholarship rather than assigning a witness's scribal date or language directly to the lost translation.
Test whether documentary, patronage, or network evidence narrows the broad late-medieval English colonial context.
Retain 1410 only as a terminus ante quem from the current Rawlinson manuscript date until stronger evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: When and where in Ireland was the anonymous vernacular translation and adaptation made?

next_steps: Compare all surviving English versions with the recensio and manuscript evidence for Expugnatio Hibernica.
Keep Dimock's printed Latin edition as Furnivall's editorial comparison source, not as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.
Do not promote Furnivall's resemblance to a particular Latin version into direct descent without a modern stemmatic argument.
openQuestions.question: Which intermediate recension or lost Latin exemplar underlies the English Conquest?

next_steps: Ask TCD catalogers for the sources behind the MS 592 title and description.
Trace older TCD catalogs and scholarship for Thomas Bray and Dowdall attribution history.
Continue to separate James Yonge's adjacent Secreta Secretorum translation from authorship of the English Conquest.
openQuestions.question: What is the documentary basis, if any, for attributing the English Conquest to Thomas Bray or associating it with a translation reportedly made for Primate Dowdall in 1551?

next_steps: Build a current witness census with exact shelfmarks, loci, dates, completeness, and stable catalog identifiers.
Distinguish full copies, excerpts, later transcripts, and related notes before extending the part model.
Compare chapter boundaries and omissions without treating Furnivall's supplied chapter heads as authorial structure.
openQuestions.question: How do all eight reported surviving English texts relate to one another, and are the two complete EngConIre witnesses representative of the full vernacular tradition?

next_steps: Request a current foliation and contents statement from the Bodleian or inspect the codex directly.
Check any supplied images against Furnivall's and the XML's folio milestones.
Keep ff. 1r–28r labeled as an edition-and-encoding inference until confirmed from the manuscript or a current catalog.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current cataloged folio locus of the English Conquest in MS. Rawl. B. 490?

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship places the vernacular production in Ireland; no town, institution, or patronal household has been securely identified.
The work was adapted for a late-medieval English colonial readership in Ireland; audience and place of copying remain distinct questions.

placeStatements.placeLabel: English colonial community in late medieval Ireland

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship treats the English text as an anonymous translation and adaptation; Gerald of Wales authored the Latin source, not this English wording.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter

assertion.notes: The vernacular work abridges, rearranges, omits, and expands its Latin source rather than reproducing it word for word.

scope.description: Furnivall's 1896 two-witness parallel-text edition and its retained editorial framing
Transcription of the TCD MS 592 main version and the short TCD MS 593 comparison specimen
Working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490 used by Furnivall
Collation of the edition proofs against MS. Rawl. B. 490

assertion.notes: The present record does not accept Thomas Bray as author. The catalog wording conflicts with the anonymous-work treatment in modern scholarship and has not been independently substantiated.

notes: James Yonge is not assigned any responsibility for this work: his Secreta Secretorum translation begins as adjacent content in MS 592 and is separately present in MS. Rawl. B. 490.

scope.description: Current catalog wording for TCD MS 592, retained only as attribution history

subjects.label: Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland, 1166–1185
English colonial community and identity in medieval Ireland

summaries.value: The English Conquest of Ireland is an anonymous Hiberno-Middle English prose retelling of Gerald of Wales's Expugnatio Hibernica. It narrates the Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest from 1166 to 1185 for a late-medieval English readership in Ireland. The translator worked from an unidentified intermediate recension rather than a securely recovered Latin exemplar and reshaped the source through condensation, omission, reordering, and expansion while preserving its broad historical sequence and colonial perspective.

EngConIre encodes two complete manuscript versions of the same work: TCD MS 592 and MS. Rawl. B. 490. Furnivall printed them on facing pages, but the Michigan XML serializes the TCD version first and the Rawlinson version second. Its 124 chapter DIV2 elements therefore represent 62 editorial chapter positions repeated across two versions, not 124 independent works or necessarily 124 authorial divisions. A short TCD MS 593 specimen in the foreword and Furnivall's terminal note remain editorial paratext. The 1969 reprint's glossary and indexes were expressly excluded from the Michigan encoding.

The two printed witness streams are substantially parallel but are not interchangeable. Furnivall explains that each supplies omissions in the other and chose the Rawlinson text as closer to the standard written language. The XML preserves both streams, their page sequence, and most witness folio milestones, but some chapter headings, supplied passages, punctuation, and folio-marker placements are editorial judgments.

notes: No authorial title for the lost translation has been verified; this concise discovery title derives from the editorial and catalog tradition.
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloging, following the established edition and catalog title

notes: This is the 1896 edition title and supplies the work's conventional modern title and historical range; it is not claimed as a manuscript or authorial title.
